translation: Whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth exalts Allah, and He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
# Surah As-Saff

## Ayah 1

**سَبَّحَ لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ**

*Everything in the heavens and the earth glorifies Allah, and He is the Almighty, the All-Wise.*

This surah opens with a powerful declaration of tasbih—the glorification of Allah ﷻ by all of creation. The ayah states that everything in the skies and the earth has been declaring Allah's perfection, and He is the ultimate authority, all wise.

## Understanding Tasbih

The word سَبَّحَ (Sabbaha) comes from the root سَبَحَ (sabaha), which literally means to swim or to float, to stay afloat. When something floats, it remains where it is—it doesn't sink, it doesn't drown. The concept of tasbih carries this same meaning: to make something swimming, to keep it elevated and afloat.

The tasbih of Allah ﷻ means to say things about Him that are befitting of His majesty. We declare Allah's perfection, His greatness, His mercy, His creative power, His absolute uniqueness. We maintain this elevated status for Him and refuse to say anything that would let that status sink or diminish. Tasbih is not merely praising Allah ﷻ—it is protecting His status from being lowered by inappropriate statements or beliefs.

To perform tasbih is to refuse to say something that is beneath Allah ﷻ, something that isn't becoming of Him, something that isn't appropriate for His divine nature. When people make inappropriate claims about Allah ﷻ, the Quran responds with this very concept. Allah ﷻ declares: سُبْحَانَهُ وَتَعَالَىٰ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ عُلُوًّا كَبِيرًا—"Glorified and Exalted is He above what they say, by great sublimity." And سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ وَتَعَالَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ—"Glory be to Allah, and exalted is He above whatever they associate with Him." He is way above such allegations. His status must be maintained. That is tasbih—a constant declaration of Allah's perfection and His being free from any flaw or deficiency.

## Tasbih: A Responsibility of Creation

Tasbih is more about us maintaining the grandeur of Allah ﷻ as we should. While some of Allah's names describe His eternal attributes, this particular form of remembrance is always connected back to the creation itself. Even the phrase we say—SubhanAllah—contains an implied verb. The complete meaning is "We declare the perfection of Allah" or "We announce the perfection of Allah." The emphasis is on the action of the creation, not on any need in the Creator. We must do everything we can not to say something that is beneath Allah's perfection.

## The Deep Meaning in This Surah

In this particular surah, the concept of tasbih takes on a profound significance. Everything in the universe is maintaining Allah's status as it should. The ayah concludes by reminding us that He is الْعَزِيزُ (Al-'Aziz)—the ultimate authority, the One whose power cannot be challenged. Why wouldn't all of creation glorify Him? And He is الْحَكِيمُ (Al-Hakim)—the One full of wisdom, whose every decree and action is based on perfect knowledge and perfect judgment.

The message is clear: all of creation naturally recognizes and declares Allah's perfection. The question this surah will explore is whether human beings, gifted with choice and consciousness, will align themselves with this universal reality.

translation: O you who have believed, why do you say what you do not do
commentary:
**يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَ تَقُولُونَ مَا لَا تَفْعَلُونَ**

*O you who believe, why do you say what you do not do?*

But now Allah ﷻ turns His attention to humanity. We declare His perfection with our tongues, yet we often don't live by it. We say *Allahu Akbar* - Allah is Greater - but is He truly the greatest authority in our lives? We seek His forgiveness with *astaghfirullah*, then act as though we don't need that forgiveness, as though we're already forgiven without genuine repentance. We declare His perfection, then do things that clearly contradict His teachings.

This is particularly significant because we are supposed to be His *khulafa' fil-ard* - those left behind on Earth to be an example. There is a complaint from Allah ﷻ in this ayah that should shake us to our core.

Before we dismiss this as only applying to the hypocrites of Madinah, let's take an introspective look at what *lima taquluna ma la taf'alun* means for you and me. It's easy to turn a blind eye to our own contradictions.

Consider: in every single salah, don't we turn to Allah ﷻ and say *ihdinas-siratal-mustaqim* - guide us to the straight path? When you ask someone for something, it's because you need it, because you're going to use it. Yet we ask Allah ﷻ for guidance, then complete the cardiovascular exercise of prayer without any spiritual dimension, exit the masjid, and go directly back into disobedience.

Why do we ask Him for guidance if we don't truly care for it? And this isn't about disobeying Allah ﷻ in matters we don't know about. Even someone running a liquor store takes a break to stand for prayer and says, "Guide me to the straight path, I need it." Before even asking for guidance, he makes a declaration to Allah ﷻ in Surah Al-Fatihah: *iyyaka na'budu wa iyyaka nasta'in* - "You alone we worship, and You alone we ask for help." He proclaims complete enslavement and obedience to Allah ﷻ, then asks, "Guide me, therefore." Then he returns to gross violation of that very slavery without a second thought.

This dual life, this hypocrisy, is so disgusting to Allah ﷻ that He describes it using extremely strong language in the next ayah.

translation: Great is hatred in the sight of Allah that you say what you do not do
commentary:
**كَبُرَ مَقْتًا عِندَ اللَّهِ أَن تَقُولُوا مَا لَا تَفْعَلُونَ**

*It is most hateful to Allah that you say what you do not do.*

*Kabura maqtan 'indallah* - it is hugely disgusting, intensely hateful in the sight of Allah ﷻ that you say what you don't do. The word *maqt* refers to one of the most disgusting social practices found in Arab history.

*Maqt* was a pre-Islamic practice where when a tribesman with several wives died, his oldest son from his first wife would claim: "I will inherit my father's wealth, and therefore I will also inherit my stepmothers." He would then marry all of his stepmothers. This abhorrent practice was called *maqt*, and Islam put an absolute end to it. For Allah ﷻ to use this term to describe the gap between our words and actions shows just how serious this matter is.

translation: Indeed, Allah loves those who fight in His cause in a row as though they are a [single] structure joined firmly
commentary:
**إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الَّذِينَ يُقَاتِلُونَ فِي سَبِيلِهِ صَفًّا كَأَنَّهُم بُنْيَانٌ مَّرْصُوصٌ**

*Indeed, Allah loves those who fight in His cause in rows, as though they are a cemented structure.*

Now Allah ﷻ presents the opposite picture. After condemning those who say what they don't do, He praises those whose actions align perfectly with their commitment: *innallaha yuhibbu alladhina yuqatiluna fi sabilihi saffan* - "Indeed, Allah loves those who fight in His path in organized rows."

Why is fighting mentioned here? Because obedience to Allah ﷻ always involves struggle. There's always reluctance within us. Your laziness gets in the way of getting up for Fajr. Your temptations prevent you from lowering your gaze. Your greed interferes with earning income in a halal way. There are urges inside us - part of our physical being - that keep our souls from flourishing. We continue to disobey Allah ﷻ by giving in to the demands of our bodies and our lower selves.

But of all the things we want to preserve - our addictions, our desires, our appetites - at the very top sits the preservation of life itself. If there's no life, there's no discussion of desires. So of everything you and I might be willing to sacrifice for Allah's ﷻ sake, the ultimate sacrifice would be life itself.

When Allah ﷻ says that if you're truly ready to give yourself up, He mentions the final stage: fighting in His path. Those who are ready to die for His cause - that's *yuqatiluna fi sabilihi*.

But notice: He says *saffan* - in rows. Allah ﷻ highlights the critical importance of discipline. Where else do we find *saff* in Islam? In salah. Prayer itself, in some sense, resembles military discipline. There's a leader in front, everyone follows. He makes a motion, you make that motion. The only other place you see this kind of regiment is in a military parade where the commander says "march" and you march, "stop" and you stop.

This level of discipline is embedded into our very worship. One of the fundamental things embedded into the Muslim psyche should be the idea of being an orderly, organized people. Discipline by definition means respect for time - doing the right thing at the right time, down to the second. When soldiers are told to fire, they fire simultaneously; you hear one gunshot even though twenty-five guns fired.

Salah teaches us this. Every major act of worship in Islam is tied directly to discipline: Here's the time for Fajr, here's the time for Dhuhr, here's the time for fasting to begin, here's the time to break the fast - down to the second. Here is the season for Hajj. The idea of discipline and organization is embedded into this surah and made a part of iman itself.

On one hand, there are people who say what they don't do. On the other hand, there are people so organized and committed that they're ready to fight for Allah's ﷻ sake in disciplined rows, *ka'annahum bunyanun marsus* - as though they are a cemented structure.

Let's examine this powerful metaphor. A wall is made of bricks held together by cement or mortar. Every brick has its place - some underground in the foundation, some above ground, some to the right, some to the left. None should move from their designated position. If you pile bricks together without cement holding them, is that a sustainable structure? Of course not.

Similarly, Muslims can't just have unity in name only. We must be bonded together seriously, tied together. The bond between us is the Word of Allah ﷻ - *habl Allah* - and that's what truly unites the believers. Every Muslim in the struggle for Islam knows their place, like every brick in the wall. Everyone understands the role they have to play. They're not necessarily interested in being promoted or climbing ranks or seeking higher positions. They're interested in doing the best they can in their assigned role.

That's the essence of discipline in an organized community: everybody knows what they're supposed to be doing, the role they're meant to play, the contribution they should be making. And they are bonded together with others, unified in purpose.

This bonding through struggle is natural. When people make Hajj together, they form lifelong friendships. When you struggle alongside someone, you become brothers for life. People who survive disasters together - a rough plane landing, a shipwreck, a calamity - even if they were strangers before, they become bonded for life afterward.

This is what Allah ﷻ wants from His ummah: not just individual believers, but a cemented structure where each person fulfills their role with excellence while being unshakably bonded to others through the rope of Allah ﷻ. Only such a community can truly say what they do and do what they say.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when Moses said to his people, "O my people, why do you harm me while you certainly know that I am the messenger of Allah to you?" And when they deviated, Allah caused their hearts to deviate. And Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ لِمَ تُؤْذُونَنِى وَقَد تَّعْلَمُونَ أَنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا زَاغُوٓا۟ أَزَاغَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ

*And [recall] when Musa (AS) said to his people, "O my people, why do you harm me while you certainly know that I am the messenger of Allah ﷻ to you?" And when they deviated, Allah ﷻ caused their hearts to deviate. And Allah ﷻ does not guide the defiantly disobedient people.*

Now the Surah shifts to historical examples, beginning with Prophet Musa (AS). Notice how he addresses his nation: "يَـٰقَوْمِ" (O my people). He calls them "my nation" because to be from a nation, your father must be from that nation. This is an important detail that will be contrasted later.

When Musa (AS) asked, "لِمَ تُؤْذُونَنِى" (Why do you cause me pain?), he was addressing their entire pattern of disobedience. Why the hurtful words? Why the disorganization? Why don't you simply listen and obey? All of this caused him pain.

The critical phrase here is: "وَقَد تَّعْلَمُونَ أَنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُمْ" (And you already know very, very well that I am in fact the messenger of Allah ﷻ sent to you). They had no excuse. The proof was clear before them.

"فَلَمَّا زَاغُوٓا۟ أَزَاغَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ" (When they deviated, Allah ﷻ caused their hearts to deviate) - This reveals a profound spiritual reality. Not following the discipline of the messenger directly impacted their hearts. They deviated from his command, and Allah ﷻ turned their hearts away as a consequence. The deviation of action led to the deviation of the heart.

Allah ﷻ does not guide the corrupt nation - those who persist in their defiance and corruption of character. This ayah establishes a pattern that will be echoed in the next example.

translation: And [mention] when Jesus, the son of Mary, said, "O children of Israel, indeed I am the messenger of Allah to you confirming what came before me of the Torah and bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me, whose name is Ahmad." But when he came to them with clear evidences, they said, "This is obvious magic
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِنِّى رَسُولُ ٱللَّهِ إِلَيْكُم مُّصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَمُبَشِّرًۢا بِرَسُولٍۢ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِى ٱسْمُهُۥٓ أَحْمَدُ ۖ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌۭ مُّبِينٌۭ

*And [mention] when Isa (AS), the son of Maryam (AS), said, "O Children of Israel, indeed I am the messenger of Allah ﷻ to you confirming what came before me of the Torah and bringing good tidings of a messenger to come after me, whose name is Ahmad." But when he came to them with clear evidences, they said, "This is obvious magic."*

The Surah now moves from the first messenger to Bani Israel - Musa (AS) - to the last messenger to them - Isa (AS). The entire legacy, from beginning to end, is captured in these two examples.

Notice the linguistic precision: Isa (AS) says "يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ" (O Children of Israel), not "O my people" as Musa (AS) did. Why? Because Isa (AS) didn't have a father in the conventional sense, so he couldn't claim them as "his nation" in that way. This detail is preserved in divine speech. The very name "Bani Israel" (Children of Israel) already establishes that nationality comes from the father - they identify themselves by their patriarch.

Isa (AS) states: "مُّصَدِّقًۭا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ" - confirming what is "بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ" (between both my hands), meaning right in front of him. The Torah wasn't hidden from him or inaccessible. This phrase indicates the Torah was directly available to him, taught to him by Allah ﷻ Himself, not through rabbis. By the time of Isa (AS), the revelation of Musa (AS) had been corrupted by the rabbis - some parts remained, but much was kept classified, accessible only to their most elite scholars. Yet Isa (AS) had direct access to its original truth.

Then comes the momentous announcement: "وَمُبَشِّرًۢا بِرَسُولٍۢ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِى ٱسْمُهُۥٓ أَحْمَدُ" - I've come to give you good news of a messenger who will come much after I do. His name is Ahmad.

Why "Ahmad" and not "Muhammad"? The Quran mentions "Muhammad" four times but "Ahmad" only once - here, on the tongue of Isa (AS). The name Ahmad carries profound meaning. It comes from the root ح-م-د (hamd - praise). Ahmad can mean the one who praises more than anyone else - he does more hamd than anyone else. But Ahmad can also mean the one who is praised more than anyone else (from mahmud - the praised one). Both meanings are embedded in the name.

Consider the beautiful irony: this announcement comes from Isa (AS), whose nation became obsessed with the concept of praise after they went astray - "Praise the Lord," "Praise Jesus," they became a nation of praise. Yet the one they praise says: There's someone coming who will be praised even more. His name is Ahmad. He praises Allah ﷻ more than anyone, and he is praised more than anyone.

"فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌۭ مُّبِينٌۭ" - When he came to them with the clearest proofs, they said: This is obvious magic. Despite the clarity of the message and the signs, their response was immediate rejection and false accusation.

translation: And who is more unjust than one who invents about Allah untruth while he is being invited to Islam. And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people
commentary:
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُوَ يُدْعَىٰٓ إِلَى ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

*And who is more unjust than one who invents a lie about Allah ﷻ while he is being invited to Islam? And Allah ﷻ does not guide the wrongdoing people.*

Who could be a worse wrongdoer than someone who fabricates lies against Allah ﷻ while simultaneously being invited to Islam? The person stands at the threshold of guidance, hearing the call to truth, yet chooses instead to invent falsehoods about Allah ﷻ. This is the height of injustice - not just rejecting truth, but actively working against it through lies and slander.

Allah ﷻ does not guide such a wrongdoing nation. This echoes the earlier statement about the corrupt nation (الْفَـٰسِقِينَ) and establishes that persistent wrongdoing blocks one from divine guidance.

translation: They want to extinguish the light of Allah with their mouths, but Allah will perfect His light, although the disbelievers dislike it
commentary:
يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِـُٔوا۟ نُورَ ٱللَّهِ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَٱللَّهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ

*They want to extinguish the light of Allah ﷻ with their mouths, but Allah ﷻ will perfect His light, although the disbelievers dislike it.*

The imagery here is striking: "يُرِيدُونَ لِيُطْفِـُٔوا۟ نُورَ ٱللَّهِ بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ" - they want to blow out the light of Allah ﷻ with their mouths. Picture someone trying to blow out a candle - that's the verb used here. But what does it mean to try to extinguish divine light with their mouths?

It means through their propaganda, their lies, their scheming, their accusations, their false messaging. They want to blow out the light of Allah ﷻ through their words and campaigns. Every manifestation of Islam on earth is light - a believer praying salah at a gas station is light, someone reciting Quran is light, a woman walking down the street in hijab is light. These are all manifestations of the light of Islam.

And the disbelievers? They despise this light. The word used at the end - "ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ" (those who do kufr) - literally refers to those who bury. When you bury something, it goes into darkness. So those who bury the truth in darkness hate any manifestation of this light. Among them are those who can't stand seeing a Muslim, can't stand knowing their neighbors are Muslims, can't stand that someone owns a restaurant and is Muslim, absolutely cannot tolerate that a mosque might be built in their town. They're disgusted by the very idea. They hate and abhor any spread of this light.

But then Allah ﷻ makes a powerful declaration: "وَٱللَّهُ مُتِمُّ نُورِهِۦ" - Allah ﷻ will complete His light. Not just protect it, but complete it. Despite those who try to bury the light in darkness, the light will not be hidden. The light of truth will not be extinguished. It will keep growing and spreading until it reaches completion.

This ayah contains another beautiful element of wisdom: the light of Allah ﷻ spreads little by little by little until He completes it. Every effort for the sake of Islam is a small piece of this light. Every masjid, every halaqah, every teacher of Quran, every humanitarian effort, every social initiative, every small thing Muslims are doing in whatever capacity - it's all a bit of this light. And Allah ﷻ has taken it upon Himself to complete it. We contribute our small pieces, but the completion? That's Allah's promise. That's His work, not ours: "وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ" - even though the disbelievers hate it.

translation: It is He who sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth to manifest it over all religion, although those who associate others with Allah dislike it
commentary:
هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ

*It is He ﷻ who sent His Messenger ﷺ with guidance and the religion of truth to manifest it over all religion, although those who associate others with Allah ﷻ dislike it.*

"هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُۥ بِٱلْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ" - He is the One who sent His Messenger ﷺ with guidance (الْهُدَىٰ) and the religion of truth (دِينِ ٱلْحَقِّ). Two things accompany this Messenger ﷺ: divine guidance that shows the way, and a complete way of life built on truth.

"لِيُظْهِرَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلدِّينِ كُلِّهِۦ" - So that He may manifest it over all other ways of life. The word "يُظْهِرَ" means to make manifest, to make it overshadow, to make it prevail. This messenger was sent to make the deen victorious, to establish truth over all other systems.

This ayah has inspired extensive discussion throughout Islamic history about the nature of Islam's mission in the world. It speaks to Islam's concern for establishing justice - social justice, economic justice, political justice. These concerns are deeply embedded throughout the Quran. You cannot read the Quran from beginning to end without recognizing that Islam cares about justice in all its forms.

But how does this manifest in our time? What does Allah ﷻ want from Muslims today? What struggle is required of us? These questions require careful thought and consideration. The world has changed dramatically - society is organized differently than ever before in human history. We cannot simply superimpose historical models without accounting for new realities and factors that didn't exist in previous eras.

What remains constant? For every believer, it means supporting legitimate Islamic efforts - educational initiatives, humanitarian work, awareness campaigns. These are all part of the light that spreads little by little. Each person contributes according to their abilities and circumstances, knowing that Allah ﷻ has promised to complete His light.

The ayah concludes: "وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُشْرِكُونَ" - even though those who commit shirk hate it. Just as the previous ayah mentioned the disbelievers (كَـٰفِرُونَ) hating the completion of Allah's light, this ayah mentions those who associate partners with Allah ﷻ (مُشْرِكُونَ) hating the manifestation of the true religion. Yet their hatred cannot stop what Allah ﷻ has decreed. The truth will prevail, the light will complete, and the deen will manifest - regardless of who opposes it.

translation: O you who have believed, shall I guide you to a transaction that will save you from a painful punishment
[It is that] you believe in Allah and His Messenger and strive in the cause of Allah with your wealth and your lives. That is best for you, if you should know
commentary:
تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتُجَاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِأَمْوَالِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

This ayah presents the essential components of genuine faith: belief in Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, coupled with the willingness to struggle in Allah's path with both wealth and life itself. The call to جهاد (jihad) - struggle in Allah's path - encompasses a profound commitment that includes being ready to lay down one's life for the sake of Islam when circumstances demand it.

This mentality of ultimate sacrifice cannot be separated from Islamic faith. When a believer truly sells themselves to Allah ﷻ, they acknowledge that their real life, their eternal life, exists with Him. This understanding shaped the consciousness of the early Muslims and continues to shape genuine Islamic commitment today. The Prophet ﷺ himself would supplicate: *Allahummar-zuqna shahadatan fi sabilik* - "O Allah, grant us martyrdom in Your path." This dua reflects the Islamic spirit of being willing to sacrifice everything for Allah's cause.

However, understanding what this struggle truly means is absolutely critical. The willingness to sacrifice one's life in Allah's path must be grounded in proper Islamic knowledge and guided by authentic scholarship. This is not an invitation to reckless violence or terrorist acts that harm innocent people. Strapping explosives to oneself and killing civilians has no relationship to the concept of martyrdom in Islam. Such actions fundamentally contradict Islamic teachings about the sanctity of life and the rules of engagement even in legitimate warfare.

The distinction between genuine sacrifice in Allah's path and distorted interpretations that lead to terrorism must be clearly established through proper Islamic education. This is why discussing and understanding what jihad actually means in Islamic law and ethics is essential for the Muslim community today.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder: "That would be better for you if in fact you knew." True knowledge leads to proper action. Understanding the real meaning of struggling in Allah's path, supporting His religion with wealth and effort, and being willing to sacrifice for His sake when legitimately required - this is what benefits the believer in this life and the next.

translation: He will forgive for you your sins and admit you to gardens beneath which rivers flow and pleasant dwellings in gardens of perpetual residence. That is the great attainment
commentary:
يَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ وَيُدْخِلْكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ وَمَسَاكِنَ طَيِّبَةً فِي جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ ۚذَٰلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ

He will forgive you your sins and admit you to gardens beneath which rivers flow and pleasant dwellings in gardens of perpetual residence. That is the great attainment.

This ayah presents three extraordinary promises from Allah ﷻ for those who believe and engage in striving for His cause. The first is the covering and forgiveness of sins. When Allah ﷻ says He will cover your sins, He is promising complete concealment and forgiveness of our wrongdoings.

The second promise takes us to paradise itself: gardens beneath which rivers flow. This is the recurring description of Jannah throughout the Quran, emphasizing the beauty and perpetual freshness of this eternal abode.

But then Allah ﷻ adds something more specific and intimate: beautiful homes. The word مَسَاكِنَ طَيِّبَةً (masakina tayyibah) refers to dwellings that are thoroughly good and pure. The term طَيِّبَةً (tayyibah) carries meanings of goodness, pleasantness, purity and excellence. These aren't just homes—they're exceptional residences that evoke awe. You walk into these dwellings and immediately recognize their extraordinary nature. The beauty, comfort and perfection of these homes surpass anything we could imagine in this world.

What makes this even more remarkable is that these homes aren't singular. The plural form tells us that each person will have multiple beautiful residences in Jannah. One magnificent home after another, all belonging to you, all located in جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ (Jannaat Adn)—the Gardens of Eden, the Gardens of Perpetual Residence.

The name عَدْنٍ (Adn) itself comes from the root meaning to settle, to remain permanently, to reside forever. This isn't a temporary vacation home or a place you visit. This is your eternal dwelling, your permanent residence that will never end.

Allah ﷻ concludes this ayah by declaring: ذَٰلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ (Dhalika al-fawzu al-azeem)—That is the ultimate success. That is the great attainment. Everything described in this ayah—the forgiveness, the gardens, the beautiful homes, the eternal residence—this represents true success. Not temporary worldly achievements that fade away, but permanent, everlasting triumph that never diminishes.

translation: And [you will obtain] another [favor] that you love - victory from Allah and an imminent conquest; and give good tidings to the believers
commentary:
وَأُخْرَىٰ تُحِبُّونَهَا ۖ نَصْرٌ مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَفَتْحٌ قَرِيبٌ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Allah ﷻ continues by saying, "and secondarily, something you people love." After promising the believers the eternal gardens of Paradise with homes they could never have imagined, He ﷻ adds: نَصْرٌ مِّنَ اللَّهِ (Nasrum Min Allah) - help from Allah and victory that is near.

The believers naturally love victory. But Allah ﷻ wants us to understand something profound about priorities. What is victory in this world compared to the houses in Jannah? When we examine what Masakin means - those blessed dwellings in Paradise - we realize this isn't even a comparison. The contrast between achieving victory in this temporary world versus earning Paradise in the eternal next life is incomparable.

This is Allah's ﷻ message to those who struggle in His cause: don't lose sight of what you're really working toward. He ﷻ knows we love victory here, in this life. But He ﷻ loves Jannah for us. That's the greater prize, the ultimate success that should remain at the forefront of our minds.

Yet in His infinite mercy, Allah ﷻ adds, "Oh, by the way, just so you know - you love victory and help from Allah? That's coming too." He ﷻ concludes the ayah with وَبَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ - and congratulate the believers. Victory is on its way.

This is the beautiful balance Allah ﷻ establishes: strive for the eternal reward, but know that worldly success will accompany your sincere efforts as well. Keep your eyes on Jannah, and Allah ﷻ will grant you both.

translation: O you who have believed, be supporters of Allah, as when Jesus, the son of Mary, said to the disciples, "Who are my supporters for Allah?" The disciples said, "We are supporters of Allah." And a faction of the Children of Israel believed and a faction disbelieved. So We supported those who believed against their enemy, and they became dominant
commentary:
# Surah As-Saff: Ayah 14

## Ayah 14

يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا أَنصَارَ اللَّهِ كَمَا قَالَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ لِلْحَوَارِيِّينَ مَنْ أَنصَارِي إِلَى اللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ الْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ اللَّهِ ۖ فَآمَنَت طَّائِفَةٌ مِّن بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَكَفَرَت طَّائِفَةٌ ۖ فَأَيَّدْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَىٰ عَدُوِّهِمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا ظَاهِرِينَ

This ayah stands as the powerful conclusion to Surah As-Saff, crystallizing its central message. Allah ﷻ calls those who believe: "Be helpers of Allah" (كُونُوا أَنصَارَ اللَّهِ). The immediate question this raises is: what does it mean to become helpers of Allah ﷻ when He is Al-Ghani, the Self-Sufficient, who needs nothing from His creation?

The answer lies in understanding that helping Allah ﷻ means helping His deen, His cause, and the struggle for His sake. The Prophet ﷺ taught us to pray: "O Allah, aid whoever aids the deen of Muhammad ﷺ and make us from them, and humiliate whoever works against the deen of Muhammad ﷺ and don't make us from them." To be Allah's helpers means to support the mission of His Messenger ﷺ, to carry forward the message of Islam, and to struggle for the establishment of truth.

## The Model of Isa (AS)

Allah ﷻ then provides us with a living example of what this help looks like: "Just as Isa, the son of Maryam, said to his disciples..." Notice that Prophet Isa (AS) is mentioned twice in this ayah—a deliberate emphasis that demands our attention. This repetition is particularly striking because, on the surface, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ appears to have more in common with Musa (AS). Both led their communities in physical struggles, both faced armed opposition, both established nations. Isa (AS), by contrast, did not engage in military conflict.

Yet Allah ﷻ chooses to conclude this surah not with the example of Musa (AS), but with Isa (AS). This reveals something profound about the nature of the struggle that defines this ummah. The mission of our Messenger ﷺ encompasses both dimensions: the struggle of Musa (AS)—which involves fighting in the path of Allah when necessary—and the struggle of Isa (AS), which is fundamentally ideological and spiritual.

The surah began by addressing the reality of physical struggle, of qital (fighting) in the cause of Allah ﷻ. But by concluding with the example of Isa (AS), Allah ﷻ makes clear that this is not merely a surah about warfare. This is a surah about jihad in its comprehensive sense—the complete struggle to uphold and spread the truth. The word jihad should never be restricted to armed conflict alone. It encompasses every form of struggle undertaken for the cause of the Messenger and the message he carried.

## The Call and the Response

Isa (AS) asked his disciples: "Who will aid me towards Allah?" (مَنْ أَنصَارِي إِلَى اللَّهِ). These were simple people—fishermen, laborers, those who washed clothes by the riverbanks. They weren't scholars or warriors or nobility. But when they heard the call of their prophet, they responded with immediate conviction: "We are the helpers of Allah" (نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ اللَّهِ).

This is what it means to be among Allah's helpers: to understand the mission of your messenger and to dedicate yourself completely to fulfilling that mission. When Isa (AS) called them to aid him in reaching the pleasure of Allah ﷻ, these ordinary people understood that they were being invited into something extraordinary. They were ready to sacrifice everything—even their lives if necessary—for the message to spread. If delivering the message of truth was considered a capital crime, they were prepared to put their lives on the line. This is jihad: the willingness to struggle and sacrifice for the cause of truth.

## Division Among the Israelites

The ayah then reveals a sobering reality: "A group among the Israelites came to believe, and a group among them disbelieved" (فَآمَنَت طَّائِفَةٌ مِّن بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَكَفَرَت طَّائِفَةٌ). Notice what Allah ﷻ is highlighting here. The struggle wasn't between competing nations or external enemies. The division ran through the heart of Bani Israel themselves. Some accepted the call of Isa (AS) and became believers; others rejected it and became disbelievers.

This internal split within a single community reveals a pattern that repeats throughout history. The battle for truth isn't always between clearly defined opposing sides. Often, the deepest divisions occur within communities that share the same heritage, the same scripture, the same ancestry. The real test is: when the truth comes to you through a messenger of Allah ﷻ, will you accept it or reject it?

## The Promise of Victory

The ayah concludes with a divine promise: "Then We aided those who believed against their enemies, and they became dominant" (فَأَيَّدْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَىٰ عَدُوِّهِمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا ظَاهِرِينَ). But here's what requires deep reflection: during the lifetime of Isa (AS), did his followers become dominant? The historical answer is no. They faced persecution, oppression and marginalization. Many were martyred for their faith.

So what does Allah ﷻ mean when He says He aided them and made them dominant? The answer unfolds across multiple dimensions. First, Allah ﷻ gave them eternal victory—the ultimate triumph in the Hereafter that no worldly defeat can diminish. Second, over the course of time, the message of Isa (AS) did eventually spread across vast regions of the earth. Consider the story of the People of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf)—young believers in Isa (AS) who fled persecution. Eventually, Allah ﷻ made their story manifest, rescued their legacy, and honored their sacrifice.

This is a crucial lesson for believers in every age: victory doesn't necessarily come in a single lifetime. Don't be hasty in expecting immediate triumph. The struggle for truth operates on Allah's timeline, not ours. The companions of the Prophet ﷺ understood this deeply when they received this surah. They realized they weren't embarking on something unprecedented. This was the same struggle Musa (AS) was commanded to undertake. This was the same struggle Isa (AS) was told to pursue.

## The Pattern of History

What these surahs of the Quran offer us is a comprehensive vision for the struggle for Islam and a framework for what believers must be mentally and spiritually prepared to face. When the Companions (RA) heard these revelations, they understood that if they found themselves dragging their feet or feeling hesitant, they weren't the first. The followers of Isa (AS) experienced doubt and hesitation. The followers of Musa (AS) dragged their feet. In every generation, some believed and some disbelieved. Some answered the call and some turned away.

This is history repeating itself, and the question for every believer is: which side of history will you be on? Now that this surah has been completed and its message delivered, now that you understand the nature of the struggle and the promise of ultimate victory, will you be among those who respond like the hawariyun: "We are the helpers of Allah"?

The mission hasn't changed. The call is the same call that Isa (AS) made to his disciples, the same call that every prophet made to his people. The difference is that now, with the final Messenger ﷺ, this mission reaches its completion. To be among the helpers of Allah ﷻ today means to carry forward this eternal mission—with the patience to endure setbacks, the wisdom to recognize that victory comes in Allah's timing, and the conviction that ultimately, those who believe and struggle for His cause will be the ones who become dominant.
